attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman is reviewed in the post-anaesthetic care unit following an elective total hip replacement. She has a medical history of hypertension and osteoarthritis. Her regular medications are amlodipine and paracetamol. During the three-hour procedure under spinal anaesthesia and sedation, she received a total of cefazolin 2g, fentanyl 100 mcg, midazolam 2 mg, and 3 L of compound sodium lactate solution. Her blood pressure dropped to 85/50 mmHg intraoperatively requiring intermittent metaraminol boluses.

On examination, she appears distressed and drowsy. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 145/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. There are bilateral coarse crackles on chest auscultation. Her surgical site dressing is clean and dry. She has received ondansetron 4 mg for nausea.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of her respiratory distress?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of acute pulmonary oedema in the immediate post-operative period. The administration of 3 L of intravenous fluids over three hours is excessive for this procedure, particularly in an elderly patient who may have reduced cardiac reserve. While there are several potential causes for her respiratory distress, the clinical picture is most consistent with fluid overload.

Pulmonary oedema is the most likely diagnosis given the history of large volume fluid administration and examination findings of bilateral crackles and oxygen desaturation. Elderly patients are particularly susceptible to fluid overload due to reduced cardiac reserve and altered fluid handling. The elevated blood pressure may also contribute to the development of pulmonary oedema.

Incomplete reversal of paralysis would be unlikely as the patient received spinal anaesthesia without muscle relaxants. Additionally, the presence of bilateral crackles would not be explained by residual paralysis.

Anaphylaxis typically presents with hypotension, bronchospasm, and often cutaneous signs shortly after exposure to the triggering agent. While she received several medications that could potentially cause anaphylaxis, the timing and clinical features are not consistent with this diagnosis.

Aspiration is always a consideration post-operatively, especially when the anaesthetic used didn't involve a secure airway (eg endotracheal tube). However this would more typically present with unilateral findings initially, and the bilateral crackles are more suggestive of pulmonary oedema.

Fat embolism syndrome typically develops 24-72 hours after long bone surgery and presents with the classic triad of respiratory distress, neurological symptoms, and petechial rash. The timing and presentation are not consistent with this diagnosis.
learningPoints: Post-operative pulmonary oedema commonly presents with respiratory distress and is often due to excessive intravenous fluid administration during surgery.
Anaphylaxis to perioperative medications typically presents within minutes of exposure with respiratory distress and cardiovascular compromise.
Clinical features of fluid overload include tachypnoea, bilateral crackles, and oxygen desaturation.
references: Ostermann et al. Perioperative fluid management: evidence-based consensus recommendations from the international multidisciplinary Perioperative Quality Initiative, British Journal of Anaesthesia, Volume 133, Issue 6, 2024, Pages 1263-1275, ISSN 0007-0912, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bja.2024.07.038.
A. Miskovic, A. B. Lumb, Postoperative pulmonary complications, BJA: British Journal of Anaesthesia, Volume 118, Issue 3, March 2017, Pages 317–334, https://doi.org/10.1093/bja/aex002

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
